name: Secundum ASD
identifyBy: Crochetage sign: notch near the apex of the R wave in inferior leads II, III, aVF
Present in all three inferior leads: specificity 92-100% for secundum ASD
Incomplete RBBB pattern in V1: rSr' with narrow small r', QRS 100-120ms
Notch correlates with shunt severity - larger defect = more prominent crochetage

measurements.rhythm: Regular (or AF in older patients with longstanding ASD)
measurements.pWaves: Normal or P-pulmonale if significant shunting
measurements.qrsDuration: 100-120ms (incomplete RBBB - rSr' V1 + crochetage II, III, aVF)

management: Closure: catheter-based device closure (Amplatzer/equivalent) or surgical repair
Indications for closure: Qp:Qs ratio >1.5:1 (significant left-to-right shunt)
Small ASD with no RV enlargement: observation and follow-up
Pre-closure: echocardiogram (transoesophageal for anatomy), cardiac catheterisation for pressures
Post-closure: most ECG changes (crochetage notch, rSr', right axis) partially or fully resolve over months
Timing: ideally in childhood/adolescence before irreversible pulmonary hypertension develops
Eisenmenger syndrome (reversal of shunt due to pulmonary HTN): closure contraindicated
pearls: Crochetage sign is more specific for secundum ASD than the rSr' in V1 - the rSr' pattern also appears in incomplete RBBB and as a normal variant
Crochetage notch in all three inferior leads (II, III, aVF) has 92-100% specificity when combined with incomplete RBBB
Secundum ASD (most common): ostium secundum - closes with catheter device; rSr' V1 + right axis
Primum ASD: different ECG - left axis deviation (not right), AV septal defect - requires surgery
ASD in adults often presents with AF or gradual dyspnoea - the ECG may show AF with residual RV overload changes
Down syndrome (Trisomy 21) has high prevalence of AV septal/primum ASD - different ECG pattern (left axis)
differentials: Incomplete RBBB (rSr' V1 but NO crochetage sign in inferior leads, no right axis deviation)
Complete RBBB (QRS >120ms, slurred broad R', no crochetage)
RV hypertrophy (tall R in V1, right axis, but different cause and pattern)
Normal rSr' variant (small narrow r' in V1 only, no right axis, no inferior notching)

qaNotes: Structural/atrial enlargement pattern preserves P/QRS/ST-T morphology from source content.
